common_name: Blushing Bromeliad (Concentrica Neoregelia, Purple Heart Bromeliad, Bull's Eye Bromeliad)

planting.note: Grow in partial shade in a shallow pot with well-draining bromeliad mix or mounted epiphytically; Neoregelia concentrica is one of the largest and most dramatic of the Neoregelia bromeliads, producing broad, spreading rosettes of wide leaves (3–4 inches wide, 18–24 inches long) with the characteristic Neoregelia quality: the central cup of leaves turns from green to vivid purple, red, and pink around the time of flowering, and small blue flowers appear in the central pool of water held in the cup. Keep water in the central cup, refresh regularly to prevent stagnation. Tolerates brief freezes to 30°F when established. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in partial shade in a shallow pot with well-draining bromeliad mix or mounted on a tree branch — Blushing Bromeliad is one of the most ornamentally striking of the large Neoregelia species, producing extremely broad, spreading rosettes of flat leaves that make a bold statement in shaded garden beds and containers in zone 9-12. Neoregelia concentrica is a large, tank-forming, epiphytic bromeliad in the Bromeliaceae family native to southern Brazil — one of the parent species of many popular Neoregelia hybrids grown for their colorful, blushing inner leaves. The plant produces a broad, spreading, flat rosette of wide (3–4 inch), broadly strap-shaped leaves in dark green with a distinctive pattern of dark purple to black spotting, blotching, or banding. The most distinctive quality of Neoregelia concentrica, shared by Neoregelia species generally, is the dramatic color change that occurs around flowering time: the central leaves (the innermost ring surrounding the water-filled central cup) turn from green to vivid purple-red, pink, or burgundy — the 'blushing' effect that gives the group its common name — creating a brilliant, jewel-like central display that persists for many months. Small, lavender-blue flowers then emerge in the central water cup. Neoregelia concentrica is valued in the bromeliad trade as one of the broadest-leaved, most dramatically spotted and blushing of the species, and as a parent of many large-leaved, heavily-marked hybrids. Excellent in shaded beds, under palms and live oaks, or as epiphytic specimens on tree trunks. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Neoregelia bromeliads for Florida landscapes
Bromeliad Society International — Neoregelia concentrica cultivation notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Bromeliads for Gulf Coast zone 9 landscapes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Neoregelia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Giant Wild Pine (Swollen Tillandsia, Large Air Plant, Cardinal Air Plant)

planting.note: Wire or secure pups or seedlings to rough bark of a tree or mount on drift wood in bright indirect light; Tillandsia utriculata is the largest native Florida tillandsia, forming imposing rosettes 2–4 feet across on tree trunks in south Florida hammock and cypress forests. The plant is monocarpic — the main rosette blooms once (on a branched, 3–5 foot spike of white flowers) and then dies, but first produces several pups (offset rosettes) that will eventually bloom in their turn. Wild plants are protected in Florida. The Tillandsia utriculata weevil (Metamasius callizona) is an invasive pest that has devastated populations throughout south Florida — purchasing from reputable nurseries helps preserve wild genetic diversity. Zone 9-12. Non-toxic.

directions: Secure to rough tree bark or drift wood in bright indirect to partial direct light, misting or dunking in water 2–3 times weekly in summer, less in winter — Giant Wild Pine is best established by attaching young pups or seedlings to rough-barked substrate where they will slowly develop their root system and expand into impressive, architectural specimens. Tillandsia utriculata is a large, rosette-forming, epiphytic bromeliad in the Bromeliaceae family native to Florida (Charlotte to Dade Counties), the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central America — the largest Tillandsia species native to Florida and one of the most imposing of all native Florida epiphytes. The plant forms a broad, upright to arching, green to gray-green rosette 2–4 feet across of smooth, somewhat succulent, strap-shaped leaves that taper to a point. These broad, water-collecting leaves create a central cup that catches rainwater and organic debris, supporting a small aquatic community of tree frogs, insects, and microorganisms. When the rosette matures after several years of growth, it produces a spectacular, branched flower spike 3–5 feet tall bearing numerous small, white tubular flowers with a pleasant fragrance — a terminal bloom that exhausts the main rosette, which then dies. Before dying, the main plant produces 2–5 pups (offsets) from the base that will grow on to bloom in turn. Wild plants in Florida have been severely impacted by the invasive Mexican bromeliad weevil (Metamasius callizona), which has decimated populations throughout south Florida. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tillandsia utriculata: Florida's largest native air plant
Florida Native Plant Society — Tillandsia utriculata species profile
Bromeliad Society International — Tillandsia utriculata cultivation and conservation
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Bromeliads of Florida

common_name: Inflated Air Plant (Balbis' Tillandsia, Spreading Air Plant, Northern Needleleaf)

planting.note: Wire to rough bark or mount on drift wood in bright indirect light; Tillandsia balbisiana is a medium-sized Florida native Tillandsia with a distinctive inflated, bulb-like pseudo-stem at the base, from which spreading, curved, gray-green leaves emerge. The inflated base gives the plant a characteristic shape unlike most other air plants. Monocarpic — blooms once on a branched spike with violet-blue flowers, then produces pups and dies. Mist or dunk in water 2–3 times weekly. More cold-tolerant than many Tillandsia species. Wild plants are protected in Florida. Zone 9-12. Non-toxic.

directions: Secure to rough bark, cork, or driftwood in bright indirect to partial direct light, misting or dunking regularly — Inflated Air Plant establishes readily on rough-barked surfaces and is one of the more distinctive Florida native tillandsias for its strongly inflated, pseudobulb-like base that sets it apart from the spreading, grass-like or funnel-forming shapes of other species. Tillandsia balbisiana is an epiphytic bromeliad in the Bromeliaceae family native to Florida (Charlotte to Monroe Counties), the Caribbean (Cuba, Jamaica, Hispaniola, Puerto Rico), Mexico, Central America, and northern South America — a common and adaptable Tillandsia species across the Gulf Coast and Caribbean regions. The plant is immediately recognizable by its strongly swollen, hollow pseudo-stem base (resembling a small bulb or flask) from which the leaves emerge in a rosette pattern — the base provides water storage and is an adaptation to the seasonally dry conditions of coastal hammock environments. The leaves are narrow, channeled, curved, gray-green to silver-gray from their dense trichome (scale) covering — the typical air plant leaf type that absorbs water and nutrients from rain and atmospheric moisture. When the plant matures, a branched flower spike emerges bearing coral-pink to red bracts and small, violet-blue flowers — a monocarpic bloom after which the rosette produces pups and dies. An excellent, distinctive native tillandsia for zone 9-12 outdoor air-plant collections. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tillandsia balbisiana: native air plant for Florida
Florida Native Plant Society — Tillandsia balbisiana species profile
Bromeliad Society International — Tillandsia balbisiana cultivation notes
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Bromeliads of Florida

common_name: Ribbon Orchid (Long-Petaled Bulbophyllum, Whip Orchid, Long-Sepal Bulbophyllum)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork or in a slatted basket in bright indirect to partial direct morning light with high humidity; Bulbophyllum longissimum is grown for the extraordinary length of its lateral sepals — flowers on well-grown specimens produce thread-like sepal extensions 8–12 inches (sometimes to 20 cm) long that hang from the flower in whip-like filaments. The flowers are pale yellow to cream with pink to purple spotting and the long sepal threads, which wave gently in air movement. The inflorescence itself is only 3–4 inches in main flower size, but the hanging filaments create a dramatically different visual impact. Intermediate to warm temperatures. The flowers have a mild unpleasant odor for the first few days. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow mounted on cork bark or in a slatted basket in bright indirect to partial direct light with good humidity and air movement — Ribbon Orchid is one of the most extraordinary of the long-sepal Bulbophyllum species and is grown entirely for the remarkable visual effect of its hanging, whip-like lateral sepal filaments. Bulbophyllum longissimum is a sympodial, epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Thailand, Malaysia, and the Malay Peninsula — one of a distinctive group of Bulbophyllum species (section Cirrhopetalum or Cirrhopetalum alliance) in which the lateral sepals of the flowers are extended into long, thread-like filaments that can reach extraordinary lengths. In B. longissimum, the lateral sepal extensions are particularly dramatic: in well-grown specimens, these filaments reach 8–12 inches (to 20+ cm), hanging from the flower in graceful, whip-like threads that wave in the slightest air movement. The flower itself is 3–4 inches in the main sepal-and-petal body — pale yellow to cream with irregular pink to purple spotting — and the thread filaments emerge from the tips of the lateral sepals. The single-flower (or occasionally 2-flower) inflorescences emerge on short stems from the base of the pseudobulbs. The visual effect of several flowers in bloom simultaneously — each with its pair of long, waving white threads hanging from the flower body — is unique and unforgettable among orchids. The flowers produce a mild, unpleasant odor for the first 1–3 days of blooming to attract fly pollinators, then the odor dissipates. An extraordinary orchid for collectors interested in unusual flower forms. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Bulbophyllum longissimum cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Bulbophyllum longissimum species profile
International Bulbophyllum Society — Long-sepal Bulbophyllum (Cirrhopetalum alliance)
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Bulbophyllum (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Brown's Anthurium (Giant Velvet Leaf Anthurium, Large-Veined Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow in deep to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky aroid mix; Anthurium brownii is a very large velvet anthurium from Costa Rica and Colombia with leaves that can reach 36–48 inches in mature specimens — among the most imposing of the ornamental velvet anthuriums in terms of leaf size. The dark, blue-green to forest-green leaves have a noticeable velvety texture from surface papillae and the primary veins are paler, creating a subtle lighter-on-dark pattern. A slow-growing but very long-lived specimen plant for tropical shade gardens. Requires warm (above 60°F), humid conditions. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in deep shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, chunky aroid mix with consistently warm temperatures and high humidity — Brown's Anthurium is one of the most impressively large of the velvet anthurium species, eventually producing immense leaves in warm, stable conditions that rival A. regale in scale. Anthurium brownii is a climbing, hemiepiphytic anthurium in the Araceae family native to Costa Rica, Panama, and northwestern Colombia — a member of the section Cardiolonchium (velvet anthuriums) alongside A. crystallinum, A. regale, A. magnificum, and A. warocqueanum. Named in honor of N.E. Brown, a botanist at the Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew. The plant produces large, broadly cordate (heart-shaped) leaves that can reach 36–48 inches or more in well-established, mature specimens — placing it among the largest-leaved of the velvet anthuriums. The leaf surface is dark, saturated forest-green to blue-green, with a distinctly matte, velvety texture from the papillate (bumped) epidermal surface characteristic of the section. The primary veins (midrib and main lateral veins) are paler — silvery-green to yellowish-green — creating a moderately contrasting pattern of lighter veins on the dark ground that is less dramatically high-contrast than A. regale or A. magnificum but elegant in the subdued, refined manner appropriate to deep-shade foliage. A magnificent, slow-growing specimen plant for tropical shade gardens and large-scale interior spaces. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: International Aroid Society — Anthurium brownii cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium brownii species profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Large velvet anthuriums for tropical collections
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to cats and dogs)
